title: The Group Average That's Hiding Your Best and Worst Franchise Site
linkedin:
Ask a multi-site franchise owner for their group's total revenue and they'll answer in seconds.

Ask which single site actually generated most of the profit, and the pause tells you everything.

That's not a knock on the owner. It's what a consolidated P&L is built to do. It rolls every site into one number so it satisfies the bank, the franchisor's disclosure requirements, and a quick mental check on how the year is tracking.

What it quietly hides is the spread underneath. A five site group averaging a healthy margin can easily be two strong sites, two adequate ones, and one that's been subsidised by the others for a couple of years without anyone ever making a specific decision to keep it running that way.

Mid July is when this tends to surface, because the new financial year forces it whether an owner goes looking or not. Royalty calculations reset. Marketing fund contributions reset. Whatever capital was earmarked for a refit or a new site has to go somewhere, and "the group" isn't actually a business that can absorb that decision. Site four is a business. Site two is a different one, with different rent and a different local market.

The fix isn't a bigger reporting pack. It's a short, consistent set of site level numbers reviewed on the same cadence as the group figures, so "which site is carrying the group" becomes a five minute question instead of a research project.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#FractionalCFO #ProfitabilityAdvice #BusinessOwners
facebook:
Quick question for anyone running more than one site under the same brand: if we asked you which location made the most profit last year, could you answer without pulling the reports apart first?

Most owners can rattle off group revenue in seconds. Fewer can say, off the top of their head, which single site is quietly carrying the others. The consolidated P&L makes the whole group look fine, even when one location has been breaking even for a year or two without anyone deciding that's okay.

New financial year budgets are usually the moment this gets noticed, since marketing fund and royalty numbers reset regardless of whether anyone goes looking. Worth a proper look before the new budget gets rolled forward the same way as last year's.

#AustralianBusiness
google:
A note for anyone running more than one site under the same brand, whether that's a franchise, a multi-location practice, or a small chain of stores across Queensland, New South Wales or Victoria.

Most owners can tell you their group's total revenue for the year without much thought. Far fewer can say straight away which single site actually generated most of the profit, or which one has been quietly breaking even for a while without anyone making a clear decision about it. The consolidated report that goes to the bank or head office is built for comparison and compliance, not for showing where a location's own numbers stand on their own.

Mid July tends to bring this into focus naturally, since royalty calculations and marketing budgets reset for the new financial year regardless of whether anyone goes looking. It's a good moment to ask which site earned its share of next year's spend before that budget simply gets rolled forward the way it was last year.

If you run several sites and the group numbers have felt harder to read than they should lately, it might be worth separating each location's own performance from the average before this year's budgets are locked in. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog for how we approach this.
